item.title: LEGO Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight - Achievement/Trophy Guide
item.description:
LEGO Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight holds 52 achievements to collect , and it might take a fair few hours of your time, considering the abundance of collectibles and challenges that need to be hunted and completed.

There's a lot more to LEGO Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knigh t's 100% playthrough than you might think, which is why this guide might help you along your journey to becoming the one true Batman.

There are no missable trophies in LEGO Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight. We've also included the achievements below in order of progression.

All Achievements & Trophies in LEGO Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight

Achievement

Description

How to Get

Beware the Gray Ghost!

Complete Bruce's Gray Ghost costume

This is the starting level of the Prologue, and you simply need to find each Gray Ghost item to progress.

"A legend, Mr. Wayne."

Complete the prologue

The Prologue ends with the Infiltration mission.

A Friend

Unlock Jim Gordon

Complete "New Beginnings", which is the first mission in Chapter 1.

"Does it come in black?"

Equip a Red Brick

Enter your Characters from Options, then press Square/X to enter outfits, then press Triangle/Y to select your Red Bricks.

"This is my city."

Place a pin on the Map

After "New Beginnings", the open world is accessed. Open the map with the Options button and press X/A on any part of the map.

"Lighter, faster, more agile."

Unlock a Skill Upgrade form each category

You get Skill Bricks for completing main missions, so simply use one in Combat and one in Exploration.

Collecting Evidence

Roll the camera over 20 degrees in Photo Mode

Access Photo Mode via the Options button and roll the camera with the triggers.

"Gotham can feel a little safer."

Prevent 30 crimes

Explore Gotham and wait for a police dispatch announcement, which will tag a crime for you to stop. Repeat this 30 times.

The Strength To Fight

Reach Combat Combo of 99 hits

This is easier than you think. Use Batarangs to stun enemies, then spam Square/X to punch them to build your combo, and repeat with multiple enemies.

"I am the shadows."

Perform 100 Stealth Takedowns

Sneak behind enemies and press R1/RB to take them down silently, and repeat this 100 times. You should naturally unlock this during story missions (and replays).

Caped Defender

Perform 100 successful Counters

Press Triangle/Y when prompted in combat to counter an enemy. You'll get this naturally by playing.

One Bad Day

Complete Chapter 1

This will unlock after "The Red Hood Gang" mission.

Computer Privileges

Change the screensaver on the Batcomputer

In the Batcave, approach the Bat Computer (in Chapter 2 onwards) and interact with it to change the screensaver.

Rebuilding Gotham

Cash in a challenge in the Batcave

In the Batcave (Chapter 2+), you can head to the back right to find a Challenge Station, where you can claim Studs via challenges completed. You should have a challenge completed at this point.

Up for the Challenge

Complete 20 challenges

Check the Challenge Board in the Batcave and see which ones you have yet to complete. These are all pretty simple, and you'll get most simply by playing.

Not Just A Cave

Place 5 props or Trophies in the Batcave

Once you have access to the Batcave, you can place props on the customization plates. Simply decorate your Batcave with 5!

"I own the place."

Purchase all Batcave expansions.

There are 5 diggers around the Batcave that can be used to purchase expansions.

Never Do It For Free

Collect 30,000 Studs in a single Stud combo

This is easy, as you just need an x3 Multiplier and a Purple Stud. Or, you can get the Stud Cache (guide here) or accept multiple challenges at once.

"I'm good with calculation."

Maintain the Max Stud Combo for 60 seconds

The Max Stud Combo is x3, so keep collecting studs to make sure the multiplier doesn't start to deplete.

"Life's been good to me."

Collect 1,000,000 Studs

This can be done in total, not all at once. Simply destroy objects, complete challenges, collect Studs, and use the Stud Cache.

"No, Sir. The OTHER car."

Purchase 3 vehicles from the Batcave

At the entrance of the Batcave, you can purchase vehicles. In Chapter 2, you will already have some available to purchase for Batman and other characters as you progress.

"You either buy a hero..."

Purchase 5 items from Shops

The Bat-Mite Store opens at the start of Chapter 2 and can be found in the Batcave (or around Gotham). Simply make 5 purchases, whether that's trophies or sutis.

Theatricality and Deception.

Purchase 3 Gadget Upgrades

You can use WayneTech Caches at workbenches in the Batcave to upgrade Gadgets, so simply purchase any three upgrades (preferably for Batman for a later trophy).

"Hear me roar."

Unlock Catwoman

Catwoman unlocks via the Chapter 2 mission: Mimes.

"Do I look like I'm joking?"

Complete Chapter 2

You'll earn this achievement after completing Chapter 2's main mission: Joker.

The Boy Wonder

Unlock Robin

You will unlock Robin after completing Two-Face, the first mission of Chapter 3.

"It's not exactly a normal world."

Visit every district

There are 9 districts in Gotham City, and they're all accessible during and after Chapter 3.

"Wouldn't wanna make things too easy."

Get 5 gold medals in AR trials

Whether it's combat or racing, there are plenty of AR Trials found on the map.

"Things have improved."

Prevent each type of crime in Gotham once

Drive around Gotham City and make note of each crime you encounter, as you need to complete one of each.

"Is that like parachuting?"

Glide in 10 different wind tunnels

There are wind tunnels you can view on the map, and you need to glide through all of them.

The Dynamic Duo

Complete Chapter 3

Complete the final Chapter 3 mission: Poison Ivy.

"Let's see what Batgirl can do!"

Unlock Batgirl

Batgirl unlocks in the Chapter 4 mission: Batgirl Begins.

Cowardly and Superstitious Lot

Defeat every type of enemy once

You'll naturally fight each enemy type just by progressing through the main story.

"You're becoming quite the celebrity."

Discover 100 open-world events

Now that you have Batgirl, use her Hackarang to unlock the Towers in each district to reveal map icons and open-world events.

A Grayson Graduation

Unlock Nightwing

Nightwing unlocks via Chapter 4's Mr. Freeze mission.

An Endless Winter

Complete Chapter 4

Complete Chapter 4's Mr. Freeze mission.

Out of the Shadows

Unlock Talia

Talia unlocks via Chapter 5's Tumbler Chase mission.

Knightfall

Complete Chapter 5

Complete Arkham Asylum, Chapter 5's final mission.

"A Hero Can Be Anyone."

Complete Story Mode

Complete Chapter 6's "The League of Shadows Returns".

"Holy skill upgrades, Batman"

Unlock all skill upgrades

Every Gold Skill Brick you get, spend it on your skills until there's nothing more to spend.

"It's a big city. I can't be everywhere."

Unlock all fast travel points

You can find 9 shutter icons on the map, which are SubWayne Stations that can be activated and turned into fast travel points.

Wonderful Toys

Purchase all gadget upgrades for a single character

This is why you should use the workbench only on Batman, as you need enough WayneTech Caches to purchase all his upgrades for his Batclaw and Batarang.

"You have a name to maintain."

Open all WayneTech Caches

There are over 200 WayneTech Caches to collect across missions and Gotham City.

"The idea was to be a symbol"

Collect 25 Bat-Symbols

There are more than 25 Bat-Symbols around Gotham City, but you just need 25.

"Quite the collection, Master Bruce."

Collect all Batcave Trophies

Each main mission has a trophy, and each district has a trophy to collect, too, which involves destroying specific objects.

Too Many Questions

Complete every Puzzle Room

Complete all 31 Riddler and 30 Cluemaster Puzzle Rooms.

"Hopefully because he's busy."

Complete every side activity in Gotham

This includes every side mission, race, and puzzle room.

"This is too much power for one person."

Collect all Red Bricks

There are 23 found in missions and purchased in Batmite's Shop.

A Watchful Collector

Collect all the collectibles

Press the Options button to see how many collectibles you have left to find (it's going to be a lot).

"I'm not wearing hockey pads."

Unlock all suits

This will unlock once you manage to find all collectibles, but make sure you're purchasing them from Batmite's Shop! We also have a full guide on unlocking all suits here.

"Wanna know how I got these cars?"

Unlock all vehicles

This will unlock as you find collectibles and complete challenges, but we also have a full guide on unlock all vehicles here.

"I am Batman."

Collect all achievements

This is the Platinum Trophy that unlocks after you've completed all 51 trophies above.

LEGO Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight

Follow
Followed
Action
Adventure
Superhero
Open-World
Systems
Released
May 22, 2026
Developer(s)
TT Games
Publisher(s)
Warner Bros. Games
Franchise
LEGO Batman
PC Release Date
May 22, 2026
7 Images

WHERE TO PLAY

DIGITAL

Genre(s)
Action, Adventure, Superhero, Open-World
Powered by
item.content:encoded:
LEGO Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight holds 52 achievements to collect , and it might take a fair few hours of your time, considering the abundance of collectibles and challenges that need to be hunted and completed.

There's a lot more to LEGO Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knigh t's 100% playthrough than you might think, which is why this guide might help you along your journey to becoming the one true Batman.

There are no missable trophies in LEGO Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight. We've also included the achievements below in order of progression.

All Achievements & Trophies in LEGO Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight

Achievement

Description

How to Get

Beware the Gray Ghost!

Complete Bruce's Gray Ghost costume

This is the starting level of the Prologue, and you simply need to find each Gray Ghost item to progress.

"A legend, Mr. Wayne."

Complete the prologue

The Prologue ends with the Infiltration mission.

A Friend

Unlock Jim Gordon

Complete "New Beginnings", which is the first mission in Chapter 1.

"Does it come in black?"

Equip a Red Brick

Enter your Characters from Options, then press Square/X to enter outfits, then press Triangle/Y to select your Red Bricks.

"This is my city."

Place a pin on the Map

After "New Beginnings", the open world is accessed. Open the map with the Options button and press X/A on any part of the map.

"Lighter, faster, more agile."

Unlock a Skill Upgrade form each category

You get Skill Bricks for completing main missions, so simply use one in Combat and one in Exploration.

Collecting Evidence

Roll the camera over 20 degrees in Photo Mode

Access Photo Mode via the Options button and roll the camera with the triggers.

"Gotham can feel a little safer."

Prevent 30 crimes

Explore Gotham and wait for a police dispatch announcement, which will tag a crime for you to stop. Repeat this 30 times.

The Strength To Fight

Reach Combat Combo of 99 hits

This is easier than you think. Use Batarangs to stun enemies, then spam Square/X to punch them to build your combo, and repeat with multiple enemies.

"I am the shadows."

Perform 100 Stealth Takedowns

Sneak behind enemies and press R1/RB to take them down silently, and repeat this 100 times. You should naturally unlock this during story missions (and replays).

Caped Defender

Perform 100 successful Counters

Press Triangle/Y when prompted in combat to counter an enemy. You'll get this naturally by playing.

One Bad Day

Complete Chapter 1

This will unlock after "The Red Hood Gang" mission.

Computer Privileges

Change the screensaver on the Batcomputer

In the Batcave, approach the Bat Computer (in Chapter 2 onwards) and interact with it to change the screensaver.

Rebuilding Gotham

Cash in a challenge in the Batcave

In the Batcave (Chapter 2+), you can head to the back right to find a Challenge Station, where you can claim Studs via challenges completed. You should have a challenge completed at this point.

Up for the Challenge

Complete 20 challenges

Check the Challenge Board in the Batcave and see which ones you have yet to complete. These are all pretty simple, and you'll get most simply by playing.

Not Just A Cave

Place 5 props or Trophies in the Batcave

Once you have access to the Batcave, you can place props on the customization plates. Simply decorate your Batcave with 5!

"I own the place."

Purchase all Batcave expansions.

There are 5 diggers around the Batcave that can be used to purchase expansions.

Never Do It For Free

Collect 30,000 Studs in a single Stud combo

This is easy, as you just need an x3 Multiplier and a Purple Stud. Or, you can get the Stud Cache (guide here) or accept multiple challenges at once.

"I'm good with calculation."

Maintain the Max Stud Combo for 60 seconds

The Max Stud Combo is x3, so keep collecting studs to make sure the multiplier doesn't start to deplete.

"Life's been good to me."

Collect 1,000,000 Studs

This can be done in total, not all at once. Simply destroy objects, complete challenges, collect Studs, and use the Stud Cache.

"No, Sir. The OTHER car."

Purchase 3 vehicles from the Batcave

At the entrance of the Batcave, you can purchase vehicles. In Chapter 2, you will already have some available to purchase for Batman and other characters as you progress.

"You either buy a hero..."

Purchase 5 items from Shops

The Bat-Mite Store opens at the start of Chapter 2 and can be found in the Batcave (or around Gotham). Simply make 5 purchases, whether that's trophies or sutis.

Theatricality and Deception.

Purchase 3 Gadget Upgrades

You can use WayneTech Caches at workbenches in the Batcave to upgrade Gadgets, so simply purchase any three upgrades (preferably for Batman for a later trophy).

"Hear me roar."

Unlock Catwoman

Catwoman unlocks via the Chapter 2 mission: Mimes.

"Do I look like I'm joking?"

Complete Chapter 2

You'll earn this achievement after completing Chapter 2's main mission: Joker.

The Boy Wonder

Unlock Robin

You will unlock Robin after completing Two-Face, the first mission of Chapter 3.

"It's not exactly a normal world."

Visit every district

There are 9 districts in Gotham City, and they're all accessible during and after Chapter 3.

"Wouldn't wanna make things too easy."

Get 5 gold medals in AR trials

Whether it's combat or racing, there are plenty of AR Trials found on the map.

"Things have improved."

Prevent each type of crime in Gotham once

Drive around Gotham City and make note of each crime you encounter, as you need to complete one of each.

"Is that like parachuting?"

Glide in 10 different wind tunnels

There are wind tunnels you can view on the map, and you need to glide through all of them.

The Dynamic Duo

Complete Chapter 3

Complete the final Chapter 3 mission: Poison Ivy.

"Let's see what Batgirl can do!"

Unlock Batgirl

Batgirl unlocks in the Chapter 4 mission: Batgirl Begins.

Cowardly and Superstitious Lot

Defeat every type of enemy once

You'll naturally fight each enemy type just by progressing through the main story.

"You're becoming quite the celebrity."

Discover 100 open-world events

Now that you have Batgirl, use her Hackarang to unlock the Towers in each district to reveal map icons and open-world events.

A Grayson Graduation

Unlock Nightwing

Nightwing unlocks via Chapter 4's Mr. Freeze mission.

An Endless Winter

Complete Chapter 4

Complete Chapter 4's Mr. Freeze mission.

Out of the Shadows

Unlock Talia

Talia unlocks via Chapter 5's Tumbler Chase mission.

Knightfall

Complete Chapter 5

Complete Arkham Asylum, Chapter 5's final mission.

"A Hero Can Be Anyone."

Complete Story Mode

Complete Chapter 6's "The League of Shadows Returns".

"Holy skill upgrades, Batman"

Unlock all skill upgrades

Every Gold Skill Brick you get, spend it on your skills until there's nothing more to spend.

"It's a big city. I can't be everywhere."

Unlock all fast travel points

You can find 9 shutter icons on the map, which are SubWayne Stations that can be activated and turned into fast travel points.

Wonderful Toys

Purchase all gadget upgrades for a single character

This is why you should use the workbench only on Batman, as you need enough WayneTech Caches to purchase all his upgrades for his Batclaw and Batarang.

"You have a name to maintain."

Open all WayneTech Caches

There are over 200 WayneTech Caches to collect across missions and Gotham City.

"The idea was to be a symbol"

Collect 25 Bat-Symbols

There are more than 25 Bat-Symbols around Gotham City, but you just need 25.

"Quite the collection, Master Bruce."

Collect all Batcave Trophies

Each main mission has a trophy, and each district has a trophy to collect, too, which involves destroying specific objects.

Too Many Questions

Complete every Puzzle Room

Complete all 31 Riddler and 30 Cluemaster Puzzle Rooms.

"Hopefully because he's busy."

Complete every side activity in Gotham

This includes every side mission, race, and puzzle room.

"This is too much power for one person."

Collect all Red Bricks

There are 23 found in missions and purchased in Batmite's Shop.

A Watchful Collector

Collect all the collectibles

Press the Options button to see how many collectibles you have left to find (it's going to be a lot).

"I'm not wearing hockey pads."

Unlock all suits

This will unlock once you manage to find all collectibles, but make sure you're purchasing them from Batmite's Shop! We also have a full guide on unlocking all suits here.

"Wanna know how I got these cars?"

Unlock all vehicles

This will unlock as you find collectibles and complete challenges, but we also have a full guide on unlock all vehicles here.

"I am Batman."

Collect all achievements

This is the Platinum Trophy that unlocks after you've completed all 51 trophies above.

LEGO Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight

Genre(s)

item.title: The New Lord of the Rings Game May Have Already Dodged the Biggest Open-World Game Trap
item.description:
After denying rumors last year that suggested they were working on a Lord of the Rings game, Warhorse Studios, the team behind the Kingdom Come: Deliverance RPG series, has now confirmed that it is, in fact, currently developing a Middle-earth RPG. For me, this is some of the best news I've heard all year, as I am probably just as much a fan of The Lord of the Rings as I am of Kingdom Come: Deliverance . The open-world RPG formula Warhorse arguably perfected in its KCD series, now being used to bring Middle-earth to life, is something I've been dreaming of since I first heard the rumors, as I'm confident that if anyone can finally do Tolkien's world justice within the gaming space, it's them.

But this is about much more than Middle-earth eventually becoming an open-world playground. As a veteran Lord of the Rings Online player, I've experienced that fantasy world in a seamless space before, and I thoroughly enjoyed the time I spent in it. However, my confidence that Warhorse can deliver a Lord of the Rings open-world RPG worth playing is ultimately rooted in what I've already seen them do in Kingdom Come: Deliverance and how that can and likely will translate over to Middle-earth. It's the developer's approach to the open-world formula in general that I appreciate, and I have a feeling it's not going to abandon that philosophy and fall into the same trap that many other open-world games experience such difficulty avoiding.

Warhorse Studios Has Proven It Understands the Value of Discovery

Obviously, Warhorse Studios has proven its ability to craft an RPG that feels authentic and grounded while still offering a playable, entertaining experience. With its emphasis on historical realism in the Kingdom Come: Deliverance games, it gives players a chance to experience what life might have actually been like in the days of medieval Bohemia, from the way people dressed and spoke to the social hierarchies, religious tensions, and everyday struggles that shaped their world. But in crafting such a believable world, it has also shown how much it understands the value of discovery.

What's That Weapon?

Identify the silhouettes before time runs out.

5.0 s
0 / 10

Results

0

High Score: 0

—

More Games
What's That Weapon? Identify the silhouettes before time runs out.
Easy (7.5s)Medium (5.0s)Hard (2.5s)Permadeath (2.5s)
5.0 s
0 / 10
Results
0
High Score: 0 —
More Games

Kingdom Come: Deliverance is, first and foremost, a world that players can live in. Sure, a narrative arches over it all, but at that story's center is an ordinary protagonist. He is lovable and charming, but he was also intentionally designed not to hog the spotlight. Instead, he is more akin to the shoes players wear while they're exploring Warhorse's faithful recreation of medieval Bohemia. In that context, the open world is the main character, and Henry, the player, is just a side character trying to thrive within it.

Because of that, an immense amount of care went into ensuring Bohemia felt like a world rather than a setting—a world that felt alive, not just because of the dynamic, routine-driven NPCs that populated it, but because of what players could discover as they ventured off the beaten path. In fact, both Kingdom Come: Deliverance games go out of their way to ensure the main quest never gets in your way, resulting in a beaten path that is very easily disregarded in favor of whatever curiosity happens to pull you in the opposite direction. That's where Warhorse's open-world design shines, because discovery in Kingdom Come rarely feels like it exists to pad out a map.

So Much of What's Discoverable in a Warhorse World Isn't Marked on a Map

Unfortunately, too many open-world games fall into the trap of turning their content into a checklist of chores rather than a hidden (or somewhat hidden) list of meaningful discoveries. Maps in these games are often littered with exclamation points and other icons that essentially tell players where to go and what to do. Much of this likely comes from developers not wanting an open world that feels empty, so they avoid negative space when and where they can by shoving another activity or collectible into it. But even then, meaningless activities can make open worlds feel even more empty than they were without them. Ultimately, it all comes down to how that negative space is utilized.

To be fair, there's absolutely nothing wrong with an open-world game having an enormous number of activities for players to do. Rather, the error comes up in how it's executed. For example, Crimson Desert 's open world can take players hundreds upon hundreds of hours to complete, and yet it has a way of not making that amount of content feel overwhelming. It accomplishes this by keeping the majority of its content a mystery on the map, thereby encouraging players to discover it themselves. In turn, that frees them from the pressure of doing specific activities while simultaneously ensuring they don't feel like they're leaving anything behind by choosing a particular path.

Kingdom Come: Deliverance is, first and foremost, a world that players can live in.

Warhorse's Kingdom Come: Deliverance games are very similar, in that they trust players to find the world by paying attention to it, rather than by chasing every icon that appears on the map. Some of the most memorable moments in these games come from overhearing something strange, following a road simply because it looks interesting, wandering into a camp at the wrong time, or realizing that a seemingly ordinary NPC has a problem that can turn into a much larger story. That kind of discovery makes the world feel like a place that exists beyond the player's involvement, and for an open-world RPG, that makes all the difference.

Warhorse's Middle-earth RPG May Have Already Avoided the Open-World Trap

It's that history that tells me whatever Warhorse is cooking up in terms of a Lord of the Rings RPG is bound to avoid making Middle-earth feel like a theme park of familiar locations and map markers. That is by far the easiest trap for a game like this to fall into, especially with a world as beloved and recognizable as Tolkien's. The temptation would be to fill the map with things players already know, point them toward every major landmark, and make the whole thing feel like a guided tour through Middle-earth's greatest hits. But if Warhorse carries its Kingdom Come philosophy into this project, Middle-earth will likely end up feeling more like a place players slowly come to understand by living in it, listening to it, and letting curiosity lead them somewhere they didn't originally plan to go.

Subscribe for in-depth takes on Warhorse's Middle-earth RPG

Sign up to the newsletter for focused coverage of Warhorse's Middle-earth RPG ambitions, plus rigorous analysis of open-world design, narrative discovery, and why big adaptations succeed or fail — thoughtful game-design essays and context.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

To me, that's why Warhorse's involvement is so exciting. A Lord of the Rings open-world RPG could very easily collapse under the pressure of its own source material, especially if it mistakes scale, references, and recognizable locations for memorable exploration. Warhorse has already shown that it knows how to make an open world feel full without constantly telling players where the fullness is, and that may be exactly what Middle-earth needs. If this new game follows that same design instinct, it may have already dodged one of the genre's biggest traps before players have even seen it.
item.content:encoded:
After denying rumors last year that suggested they were working on a Lord of the Rings game, Warhorse Studios, the team behind the Kingdom Come: Deliverance RPG series, has now confirmed that it is, in fact, currently developing a Middle-earth RPG. For me, this is some of the best news I've heard all year, as I am probably just as much a fan of The Lord of the Rings as I am of Kingdom Come: Deliverance . The open-world RPG formula Warhorse arguably perfected in its KCD series, now being used to bring Middle-earth to life, is something I've been dreaming of since I first heard the rumors, as I'm confident that if anyone can finally do Tolkien's world justice within the gaming space, it's them.

But this is about much more than Middle-earth eventually becoming an open-world playground. As a veteran Lord of the Rings Online player, I've experienced that fantasy world in a seamless space before, and I thoroughly enjoyed the time I spent in it. However, my confidence that Warhorse can deliver a Lord of the Rings open-world RPG worth playing is ultimately rooted in what I've already seen them do in Kingdom Come: Deliverance and how that can and likely will translate over to Middle-earth. It's the developer's approach to the open-world formula in general that I appreciate, and I have a feeling it's not going to abandon that philosophy and fall into the same trap that many other open-world games experience such difficulty avoiding.

Warhorse Studios Has Proven It Understands the Value of Discovery

Obviously, Warhorse Studios has proven its ability to craft an RPG that feels authentic and grounded while still offering a playable, entertaining experience. With its emphasis on historical realism in the Kingdom Come: Deliverance games, it gives players a chance to experience what life might have actually been like in the days of medieval Bohemia, from the way people dressed and spoke to the social hierarchies, religious tensions, and everyday struggles that shaped their world. But in crafting such a believable world, it has also shown how much it understands the value of discovery.

What's That Weapon?

Identify the silhouettes before time runs out.

5.0 s
0 / 10

Results

0

High Score: 0

—

More Games

Easy (7.5s)Medium (5.0s)Hard (2.5s)Permadeath (2.5s)
5.0 s
0 / 10
Results
0
High Score: 0 —
More Games

Kingdom Come: Deliverance is, first and foremost, a world that players can live in. Sure, a narrative arches over it all, but at that story's center is an ordinary protagonist. He is lovable and charming, but he was also intentionally designed not to hog the spotlight. Instead, he is more akin to the shoes players wear while they're exploring Warhorse's faithful recreation of medieval Bohemia. In that context, the open world is the main character, and Henry, the player, is just a side character trying to thrive within it.

Because of that, an immense amount of care went into ensuring Bohemia felt like a world rather than a setting—a world that felt alive, not just because of the dynamic, routine-driven NPCs that populated it, but because of what players could discover as they ventured off the beaten path. In fact, both Kingdom Come: Deliverance games go out of their way to ensure the main quest never gets in your way, resulting in a beaten path that is very easily disregarded in favor of whatever curiosity happens to pull you in the opposite direction. That's where Warhorse's open-world design shines, because discovery in Kingdom Come rarely feels like it exists to pad out a map.

So Much of What's Discoverable in a Warhorse World Isn't Marked on a Map

Unfortunately, too many open-world games fall into the trap of turning their content into a checklist of chores rather than a hidden (or somewhat hidden) list of meaningful discoveries. Maps in these games are often littered with exclamation points and other icons that essentially tell players where to go and what to do. Much of this likely comes from developers not wanting an open world that feels empty, so they avoid negative space when and where they can by shoving another activity or collectible into it. But even then, meaningless activities can make open worlds feel even more empty than they were without them. Ultimately, it all comes down to how that negative space is utilized.

To be fair, there's absolutely nothing wrong with an open-world game having an enormous number of activities for players to do. Rather, the error comes up in how it's executed. For example, Crimson Desert 's open world can take players hundreds upon hundreds of hours to complete, and yet it has a way of not making that amount of content feel overwhelming. It accomplishes this by keeping the majority of its content a mystery on the map, thereby encouraging players to discover it themselves. In turn, that frees them from the pressure of doing specific activities while simultaneously ensuring they don't feel like they're leaving anything behind by choosing a particular path.

Kingdom Come: Deliverance is, first and foremost, a world that players can live in.

Warhorse's Kingdom Come: Deliverance games are very similar, in that they trust players to find the world by paying attention to it, rather than by chasing every icon that appears on the map. Some of the most memorable moments in these games come from overhearing something strange, following a road simply because it looks interesting, wandering into a camp at the wrong time, or realizing that a seemingly ordinary NPC has a problem that can turn into a much larger story. That kind of discovery makes the world feel like a place that exists beyond the player's involvement, and for an open-world RPG, that makes all the difference.

Warhorse's Middle-earth RPG May Have Already Avoided the Open-World Trap

It's that history that tells me whatever Warhorse is cooking up in terms of a Lord of the Rings RPG is bound to avoid making Middle-earth feel like a theme park of familiar locations and map markers. That is by far the easiest trap for a game like this to fall into, especially with a world as beloved and recognizable as Tolkien's. The temptation would be to fill the map with things players already know, point them toward every major landmark, and make the whole thing feel like a guided tour through Middle-earth's greatest hits. But if Warhorse carries its Kingdom Come philosophy into this project, Middle-earth will likely end up feeling more like a place players slowly come to understand by living in it, listening to it, and letting curiosity lead them somewhere they didn't originally plan to go.

Subscribe for in-depth takes on Warhorse's Middle-earth RPG

Sign up to the newsletter for focused coverage of Warhorse's Middle-earth RPG ambitions, plus rigorous analysis of open-world design, narrative discovery, and why big adaptations succeed or fail — thoughtful game-design essays and context.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

To me, that's why Warhorse's involvement is so exciting. A Lord of the Rings open-world RPG could very easily collapse under the pressure of its own source material, especially if it mistakes scale, references, and recognizable locations for memorable exploration. Warhorse has already shown that it knows how to make an open world feel full without constantly telling players where the fullness is, and that may be exactly what Middle-earth needs. If this new game follows that same design instinct, it may have already dodged one of the genre's biggest traps before players have even seen it.

item.title: Forza Horizon 6: Best Cars With Upgrade and Bodykit Presets
item.description:
Forza Horizon 6 includes some exciting new customization options that will even let you quickly tune cars in major ways. With Upgrade Presets, you can combine wide bodykits and visual parts (like rims) to match the overall style, along with various performance upgrades in just one click.

These upgrades can vary in both pricing and performance boosts , and at times, they have the potential of even being capable of pushing a car two extra classes above its default stats (for instance, from an A Class to S2), which is why we'll explain more below!

Forza Horizon 6 Upgrade Presets and Bodykits, Explained

At launch, almost 600 cars are available in the game, and each can be customized extensively, especially when it comes to performance and handling.

However, only a relatively small portion of them offer substantial visual customization parts (aside from rims, which are available for any car). These include extra spoilers, hoods, bumpers, skirts, or even full branded widebody kits.

As of today, there are around 100 widebody kits in total in Forza Horizon 6 , with some rare cars including more than one to choose from.

Some cars with bodykits may not have Upgrade Preset options available to them, which can be confusing when looking for the next car you might want to transform.

As a result, some highly customizable vehicles in FH6 , such as Mazda MX-5 Miata RF 2005, Chevrolet Corvette E-Ray 2024, and the iconic Mazda RX-7 Type R 1992, aren’t shown in the Autoshow when filtering through the “Bodykit & Presets Available” parameter.

Cheap everyday cars, especially those from the 1990-2000s era, are the best choices for extensive visual and performance customization , with brands like Honda, Nissan, Mazda, Mitsubishi, and Toyota offering the most visual options in Forza Horizon 6 .

Subscribe for In-Depth Forza Horizon 6 Tuning Guides

Join the newsletter for deeper Forza Horizon 6 coverage—detailed Upgrade Preset breakdowns, bodykit recommendations, and curated car builds so you can plan the wildest visual and performance conversions.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

If you're looking for some of the best cars primarily for visual tuning, we've listed some of the most flexible options available so far, with a focus on the wildest visual transformations currently available in Forza Horizon 6 that Japanese car culture lovers definitely won't want to miss:

FH6 Best Cars With Full Upgrade Presets Overview

Model

Default Class

Upgrade Preset Body

Upgraded Class

BMW M3 2005

B 554

Aim 9 GT

A 634

Chevy Corvette Stingray

A 699

StreetHunter Designs

S2 813

Honda S2000

B 503

Rocket Bunny

B 586

Lexus LC 500

B 592

Liberty Walk

A 663

Lambo Aventador LP700-4

S1 753

Liberty Walk

S1 798

McLaren 650S Spider

S1 765

Liberty Walk

S2 811

Nissan GT-R BE (R35)

S1 703

Liberty Walk

S1 729

Toyota GR86

B 556

Rocket Bunny

S1 704

Toyota GR Supra

A 616

HKS Grip/Drift

S2 825 (Grip), S1 783 (Drift)

Toyota Supra RZ

B 526

KRC

A 613

BMW M3 2005

  • Aim 9 GT Upgrade Preset (image)
  • Features Extra Rocket Bunny Kit
  • Autoshow Price: 35,000 CR
  • Default Class: B 554
  • Upgrade Preset Class: A 634

Chevrolet Corvette Stingray Coupe 2020

  • StreetHunter Designs Upgrade Preset
  • Autoshow Price: 65,000 CR
  • Class: A 699
  • Upgrade Preset Class: S2 813

Honda S2000 2003

  • Rocket Bunny Upgrade Preset
  • Autoshow Price: 55,000 CR
  • Class: B 503
  • Upgrade Preset Class: B 586

Lexus LC 500 2021

  • Liberty Walk Upgrade Preset
  • Autoshow Price: 79,000 CR
  • Class: B 592
  • Upgrade Preset Class: A 663

Lamborghini Aventador LP700-4 2012

  • Liberty Walk Upgrade Preset
  • Autoshow Price: Wheelspin/Auction Only
  • Class: S1 753
  • Upgrade Preset Class: S1 798

McLaren 650S Spider 2014

  • Liberty Walk Upgrade Preset
  • Autoshow Price: 130,000 CR
  • Class: S1 765
  • Upgrade Preset Class: S2 811

Nissan GT-R Black Edition (R35) 2012

  • Liberty Walk Upgrade Preset
  • Autoshow Price: 80,000 CR
  • Class: S1 703
  • Upgrade Preset Class: S1 729

Toyota GR86 2022

  • Rocket Bunny Upgrade Preset
  • Autoshow Price: 28,000 CR
  • Class: B 556
  • Upgrade Preset Class: S1 704

Toyota GR Supra 2020

  • HKS Grip/Drift Upgrade Presets To Choose (Visually Same)
  • Features Extra Forza Kit
  • Autoshow Price: 55,000 CR
  • Class: A 616
  • Upgrade Preset Class: S2 825 (Grip), S1 783 (Drift)

Toyota Supra RZ 1998

  • KRC Upgrade Preset
  • Autoshow Price: 60,000 CR
  • Class: B 526
  • Upgrade Preset Class: A 613

More Forza Horizon 6 Car Guides

To make your journey across Japan more pleasant, be sure to check out our other Forza Horizon 6 guides:

  • Best Early Game Fast Cars
  • All Treasure Cars
  • All Barn Finds
Follow
Followed
Forza Horizon 6
Racing
Open-World
Systems
9 /10
OpenCritic Reviews
Top Critic Avg: 91 /100 Critics Rec: 100%
Released
May 19, 2026
ESRB
Everyone / Mild Lyrics, Users Interact, In-Game Purchases
Developer(s)
Playground Games
Publisher(s)
Xbox Game Studios

WHERE TO PLAY

SUBSCRIPTION
DIGITAL
PHYSICAL

Genre(s)
Racing, Open-World
Powered by
item.content:encoded:
Forza Horizon 6 includes some exciting new customization options that will even let you quickly tune cars in major ways. With Upgrade Presets, you can combine wide bodykits and visual parts (like rims) to match the overall style, along with various performance upgrades in just one click.

These upgrades can vary in both pricing and performance boosts , and at times, they have the potential of even being capable of pushing a car two extra classes above its default stats (for instance, from an A Class to S2), which is why we'll explain more below!

Forza Horizon 6 Upgrade Presets and Bodykits, Explained

At launch, almost 600 cars are available in the game, and each can be customized extensively, especially when it comes to performance and handling.

However, only a relatively small portion of them offer substantial visual customization parts (aside from rims, which are available for any car). These include extra spoilers, hoods, bumpers, skirts, or even full branded widebody kits.

As of today, there are around 100 widebody kits in total in Forza Horizon 6 , with some rare cars including more than one to choose from.

Some cars with bodykits may not have Upgrade Preset options available to them, which can be confusing when looking for the next car you might want to transform.

As a result, some highly customizable vehicles in FH6 , such as Mazda MX-5 Miata RF 2005, Chevrolet Corvette E-Ray 2024, and the iconic Mazda RX-7 Type R 1992, aren’t shown in the Autoshow when filtering through the “Bodykit & Presets Available” parameter.

Cheap everyday cars, especially those from the 1990-2000s era, are the best choices for extensive visual and performance customization , with brands like Honda, Nissan, Mazda, Mitsubishi, and Toyota offering the most visual options in Forza Horizon 6 .

item.title: LEGO Batman Legacy of the Knight: Kite Man All Collectibles (Kites & Red Brick)
item.description:
Kite Man is the second mission of the third chapter. After solving several Cluemaster puzzles, players will eventually make their way to Gotham Water and Power , where Kite Man has been spotted. This LEGO Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight mission contains 5 Kites and 1 Red Brick . The Kites are well hidden, and since there is a chance you might miss one, the guide below goes over the location of every collectible​​​​​​.

For help finding collectibles and more, see our LEGO Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight collectibles hub.

Kite Man Collectibles Overview

Collectible

Objective

Location

Red Brick (Power Plant) #1

Destroy the giant plant's pods

Head down from the giant plant and make your way to the pryable door. Inside, use your gadgets to trace the battery, then move the slider to help Batman reach the Red Brick.

Kite #1

Go up the ladder and continue forward until you find the Plant Pod. The first Kite is to the right of the Pod.

Kite #2

After collecting the previous collectible, grapple up into the vents to find this one.

Kite #3

Go to the opposite side and use the gadgets to reach the door that needs to be pried open. Once opened, head inside, and the Kite will be above the doorway.

Kite #4

From the previous collectible, jump down to find this next to the furnace.

Kite #5

Head up into the vents where the Plant Pod is located, and you will find this Kite right beside it.

Red Brick (Power Plant) #1

  • After destroying the first Plant Pod, head left.
  • Instead of going up the ladder, head down toward the door that Robin can pry open.
  • As Robin, aim your gadget to trace the battery connected to the switch.
  • Move the slider so Batman can use the air vent to go up, then move the slider back afterward.
  • Collect the Red Brick up ahead.

Kite #1

  • Go up the ladder and use Batman’s X-Ray Vision.
  • Spray the explosive gel and head inside to find several crates on the left that you can break.
  • Build the mechanism that allows Robin to use his gadget and control the water pressure, allowing Batman to reach the other side.
  • To the right of the Plant Pod is the first Kite.

Kite #2

  • Before going down the pipe, turn around and look for a ledge you can grapple onto.
  • Grapple toward the vent and head inside to find the second Kite.

Kite #3

  • Go over to the opposite side and use Batman’s pull gadget.
  • Use Robin’s gadget to trace the connection and make your way toward the door that needs to be pried open.
  • Once the door opens, head inside and turn around to spot this collectible above you.

Kite #4

  • Before using your Batarang on the targets ahead, jump down.
  • Next to the furnace, you will find another Kite.

Kite #5

  • Continue upward using the air vents.
  • Inside the vent containing the Plant Pod, look to the left to find the final Kite.

All Kite Man Collectibles Rewards

  • Collecting all 5 Kites rewards you with the Kites Batcave Trophy.
  • You can also get the Power Plant Red Brick to change your outfit colors.

Don't worry if you've missed any collectibles, as you can replay missions with this guide!

Subscribe to our newsletter for complete collectibles guides

Join the newsletter to get in-depth LEGO Batman collectibles coverage - precise location notes, gadget tips, and trophy and Red Brick guidance to make sure you collect everything. Subscribe for more comprehensive collectibles guides.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

You can also check out more of our guides, where we cover all collectibles so you won't miss a thing:

  • All Infiltration Collectibles
  • All New Beginnings Collectibles
  • All Carmine Falcone Collectibles
  • All The Red Hood Gang Collectibles
  • All Mimes Collectibles
  • All The Penguin Collectibles
  • All Flugelheim Collectibles
  • All Joker Collectibles
  • All Two-Face Collectibles

LEGO Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight

Follow
Followed
Action
Adventure
Superhero
Open-World
Systems
Released
May 22, 2026
Developer(s)
TT Games
Publisher(s)
Warner Bros. Games
Franchise
LEGO Batman
PC Release Date
May 22, 2026
7 Images

WHERE TO PLAY

DIGITAL

Genre(s)
Action, Adventure, Superhero, Open-World
Powered by
item.content:encoded:
Kite Man is the second mission of the third chapter. After solving several Cluemaster puzzles, players will eventually make their way to Gotham Water and Power , where Kite Man has been spotted. This LEGO Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight mission contains 5 Kites and 1 Red Brick . The Kites are well hidden, and since there is a chance you might miss one, the guide below goes over the location of every collectible​​​​​​.

For help finding collectibles and more, see our LEGO Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight collectibles hub.

Kite Man Collectibles Overview

Collectible

Objective

Location

Red Brick (Power Plant) #1

Destroy the giant plant's pods

Head down from the giant plant and make your way to the pryable door. Inside, use your gadgets to trace the battery, then move the slider to help Batman reach the Red Brick.

Kite #1

Go up the ladder and continue forward until you find the Plant Pod. The first Kite is to the right of the Pod.

Kite #2

After collecting the previous collectible, grapple up into the vents to find this one.

Kite #3

Go to the opposite side and use the gadgets to reach the door that needs to be pried open. Once opened, head inside, and the Kite will be above the doorway.

Kite #4

From the previous collectible, jump down to find this next to the furnace.

Kite #5

Head up into the vents where the Plant Pod is located, and you will find this Kite right beside it.

Red Brick (Power Plant) #1

  • After destroying the first Plant Pod, head left.
  • Instead of going up the ladder, head down toward the door that Robin can pry open.
  • As Robin, aim your gadget to trace the battery connected to the switch.
  • Move the slider so Batman can use the air vent to go up, then move the slider back afterward.
  • Collect the Red Brick up ahead.

Kite #1

  • Go up the ladder and use Batman’s X-Ray Vision.
  • Spray the explosive gel and head inside to find several crates on the left that you can break.
  • Build the mechanism that allows Robin to use his gadget and control the water pressure, allowing Batman to reach the other side.
  • To the right of the Plant Pod is the first Kite.

Kite #2

  • Before going down the pipe, turn around and look for a ledge you can grapple onto.
  • Grapple toward the vent and head inside to find the second Kite.

Kite #3

  • Go over to the opposite side and use Batman’s pull gadget.
  • Use Robin’s gadget to trace the connection and make your way toward the door that needs to be pried open.
  • Once the door opens, head inside and turn around to spot this collectible above you.

Kite #4

  • Before using your Batarang on the targets ahead, jump down.
  • Next to the furnace, you will find another Kite.

Kite #5

  • Continue upward using the air vents.
  • Inside the vent containing the Plant Pod, look to the left to find the final Kite.

All Kite Man Collectibles Rewards

  • Collecting all 5 Kites rewards you with the Kites Batcave Trophy.
  • You can also get the Power Plant Red Brick to change your outfit colors.

Don't worry if you've missed any collectibles, as you can replay missions with this guide!

Subscribe to our newsletter for complete collectibles guides

You can also check out more of our guides, where we cover all collectibles so you won't miss a thing:

  • All New Beginnings Collectibles
  • All Carmine Falcone Collectibles
  • All The Red Hood Gang Collectibles

  • All The Penguin Collectibles

  • All Joker Collectibles

item.title: Subnautica 2: How to Get Atacamite
item.description:
Atacamite is one of the late-game ores in Subnautica 2 (in the first build of Early Access), and you will need it to craft Mangalloy Ingot , a processed metal that is used for repairing the broken turbine in the alien power plant.

Harvesting Atacamite has some initial requirements. First, you need to have the Alien Ruins region unlocked. Then, it’s highly recommended to craft and install the Depth Module on the Tadpole. The Sonic Resonator is also required, as you can’t harvest this ore with your bare hands!

Where to Find Atacamite in Subnautica 2

Atacamite spawns in several locations within the Alien Ruins’ region. You can find some nearby Metal Farms, but that’s too far away from the base.

The closest option is somewhere near the broken turbine. Start from Alien Ruins and swim towards 110 degrees until you are 250 meters away from the base. Now, move directly towards the bed of the ocean, and search the sand under the alien platform.

Atacamite only grows on the ocean’s bed . So, don’t waste your time looking for it on the cliffs or cave walls. It has a dark, spiky ore. You can craft only with a Sonic Resonator or a Feedback Resonator.

If you need this solely to fix the broken turbine, collect at least 10 units of Atacamite , as the turbine requires 10 Mangalloy ingots. Though Atacamite is only one of the three materials needed to craft that ingot.

You also need to craft Titanium ingots and collect Troilite ores. Remember, you must have a Processor at your base to craft the ingots.

What Can You Craft with Atacamite in Subnautica 2?

In Early Access, Atacamite is only used for crafting the Mangalloy ingot, which can be used for fixing the turbine . However, further updates may deliver new recipes for this dark-green ore.

You can also use Atacamite to build Metal Farms , which makes Atacamite a crucial material for this harvesting machine. The Metal Farm is considered an endgame machine in the current build of Subnautica 2, which can help you speed up the resource gathering process and build your dream bases in the depths of the ocean.

Subscribe for deeper Subnautica 2 resource guides

Level up your Subnautica 2 runs: subscribing to the newsletter gives detailed resource locations, crafting priorities, and step-by-step harvesting tactics for Atacamite and related materials, ideal for finishing endgame objectives.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Once you have enough Atacamite, fixing the turbine shouldn't be a problem if you know the whereabouts of Troilite. With this objective solved, you can enter the Observatory and learn more about the history behind the forgotten alien structures.

More Guides on Subnautica 2

Looking to find other resources in the ocean? Check these out:

  • Where to Find Celestine
  • Conduit Crystal Location
Subnautica 2
Released
May 14, 2026

WHERE TO PLAY

SUBSCRIPTION
DIGITAL

Developer(s)
Unknown Worlds Entertainment
Franchise
Subnautica
Genre(s)
Survival, Adventure
Platform(s)
Xbox Series X, Xbox Series S, PC
Powered by
item.content:encoded:
Atacamite is one of the late-game ores in Subnautica 2 (in the first build of Early Access), and you will need it to craft Mangalloy Ingot , a processed metal that is used for repairing the broken turbine in the alien power plant.

Harvesting Atacamite has some initial requirements. First, you need to have the Alien Ruins region unlocked. Then, it’s highly recommended to craft and install the Depth Module on the Tadpole. The Sonic Resonator is also required, as you can’t harvest this ore with your bare hands!

Where to Find Atacamite in Subnautica 2

Atacamite spawns in several locations within the Alien Ruins’ region. You can find some nearby Metal Farms, but that’s too far away from the base.

The closest option is somewhere near the broken turbine. Start from Alien Ruins and swim towards 110 degrees until you are 250 meters away from the base. Now, move directly towards the bed of the ocean, and search the sand under the alien platform.

Atacamite only grows on the ocean’s bed . So, don’t waste your time looking for it on the cliffs or cave walls. It has a dark, spiky ore. You can craft only with a Sonic Resonator or a Feedback Resonator.

If you need this solely to fix the broken turbine, collect at least 10 units of Atacamite , as the turbine requires 10 Mangalloy ingots. Though Atacamite is only one of the three materials needed to craft that ingot.

You also need to craft Titanium ingots and collect Troilite ores. Remember, you must have a Processor at your base to craft the ingots.

What Can You Craft with Atacamite in Subnautica 2?

In Early Access, Atacamite is only used for crafting the Mangalloy ingot, which can be used for fixing the turbine . However, further updates may deliver new recipes for this dark-green ore.

You can also use Atacamite to build Metal Farms , which makes Atacamite a crucial material for this harvesting machine. The Metal Farm is considered an endgame machine in the current build of Subnautica 2, which can help you speed up the resource gathering process and build your dream bases in the depths of the ocean.
